Offline (CLI): Scripting, CI/CD, Pre-commit Hooks, Local Development Sessions 9
Embedded (HTTP Service): Interactive IDE Plugins, Live Editing Tools, Agent Orchestration 112
Implementation Details: The CLI is optimized for non-interactive, scriptable workflows, while the HTTP server is optimized for low-latency, interactive queries.

Offline (CLI): Higher latency due to process spawning for each command.
Embedded (HTTP Service): Lower latency as the service remains resident in memory, eliminating process startup overhead 34.
Implementation Details: The HTTP server runs as a long-lived process, whereas the CLI spawns a new process for each invocation.

Offline (CLI): Single, statically-linked binary (hpc-chat-director) with no external dependencies 32.
Embedded (HTTP Service): Requires a server to be started and maintained. Can be packaged as a binary with a --serve flag.
Implementation Details: The CLI's static linking simplifies distribution immensely. The HTTP server can be deployed as a daemon or alongside an application.

Offline (CLI): Command-line arguments and standard input/output streams.
Embedded (HTTP Service): Standardized JSON over HTTP using stable schemas for requests and responses 123.
Implementation Details: The CLI uses clap for parsing. The HTTP server uses axum to define routes and serialize/deserialize JSON payloads.

Offline (CLI): Stateless per invocation; relies on command-line flags for configuration.
Embedded (HTTP Service): Stateful; can maintain connections and potentially cache results for subsequent requests.
Implementation Details: The CLI is stateless by nature. The HTTP server can implement state management, though the current design keeps it stateless for simplicity.

Offline (CLI): Easy to integrate into shell scripts and build systems.
Embedded (HTTP Service): Requires an HTTP client library. Enables tight integration with web technologies and languages with good HTTP support.
Implementation Details: The CLI integrates naturally with any system that can execute a shell command. The HTTP interface can be consumed by virtually any programming language.
